docs.title: Welcome! 👋
docs.text:
This is a daily capture of my learning journey, a canvas where each entry paints a picture of the lessons, discoveries, and insights gathered along the way. Here, I invite you join me in embracing the beauty of continuous learning.

Every day presents an opportunity to acquire new knowledge, uncover fresh perspectives, and grow. This blog serves as repository of these daily discovers, offering a glimpse into the ever-evolving tapestry of wisdom and experiences.

Whether it's a fleeting observation, a profound realization, or snippet of newfound expertise, each post encapsulates a fragment of my ongoing quest for knowledge.

I'm excited to share this voyage of learning with you to faster a community dedicated to curiosity, exploration, and growth.

Welcome to a daily capture of learning!

docs.title: About Me: Innovative Software Engineer|Expert in React, Node.js, MongoDB|Linux Enthusiast
docs.text: Hi 👋, My name is Soufiyane EL GARA, a driven software engineer with a passion for crafting exceptional digital experiences. At the heart of my expertise is my deep understanding and mastery of JavaScript, the language that fuels dynamic and interactive web applications. From front-end wizardry with React.js to build dynamic robust back-end solutions with Node.js and Express, I leverage the power of JavaScript to create seamless and intuitive user interfaces.

docs.title: My Technical Landscape:
docs.text: In addition to my JavaScript prowess, I excel in the world of Linux, navigating the Ubuntu environment with ease. I'm well-versed in scripting languages like Bash, automating tasks to enhance efficiency and streamline workflows. My toolkit also includes mastery of powerful developer tools such as Vim editor and Tmux, ensuring a productive and customized development experience. My journey into the world of containerization includes expertise in Docker and Podman, allowing me to orchestrate complex applications withe finesse.

docs.title: Collaboration and Problem Solving:
docs.text: I thrive on collaboration, understanding that the best solutions emerge from divers perspectives. I approach challenges not just as problems but as opportunities to innovate. My expertise isn't just in writing code; it's in creating solutions that not only meet requirement but exceed expectations, providing seamless user experiences and efficient workflows.

docs.title: Key skills
docs.text:
• Front-end : HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React.js
  • Back-end : Node.js, Express
  • Databases : MongoDB
  • Scripting : BASH
  • Linux : Ubuntu
  • Containerization : Docker, podman
  • Networking : Design, Optimization, Security
  • Developer Tools :Vim/VsCode Editor, Tmux
  • Methodologies : Agile Project Management, Scrum
  • Soft Skills : Strong Communication, Teal Collaboration, Problem Solving

docs.title: Why Connect?
docs.text:
I offer more than just technical skills; I bring a holistic approach software engineering. Whether you need a JavaScript expert, a Linux enthusiast, or a networking and containerization specialist, I deliver not just solutions, but innovations. Let's connect ans explore how my diverse skill set and passion for problem-solving can elevate your projects to new heights.

Feel free to reach out, and let's embark on a journey of innovations and collaboration development together!

  • 🌍 I'm based in Rabat, Morocco 🇲🇦
  • ✉️ soufiyaneelgara@gmail.com

docs.title: Learn How To Win Friends And Influence People
docs.text: By : Dale Carnegie

docs.title: Lesson I: Don't Criticize, Condemn, or Complain
docs.text:
1. Criticism is Dangerous

    • It puts people on the defensive
    • It Wounds their pride and sense of importance
    • It arouses resentment & only creates temporary changes

  2. Beware of criticizing someone, instead of solving the problems, you will only amplify them.

  3. Instead if accusing and criticizing people, try to understand them, try to see things from their perspective. This will breed sympathy, tolerance and kindness.
  4. It's easier for people to accept criticism after they're received positive feedback. So when you must criticize start with a complement and then provide constructive criticism gently and tactfully.

docs.title: Lesson II: If You Want People To Like You, Become Genuinely Interested In Them!
docs.text:
1. The easiest way to make friends is become interested in others, instead of trying to make others become interested in you.
  2. People are not really interested in you or me. They are mainly interested in themselves.
  3. If We're trying too hard to impress people and get interested in us, we will never have many real, sincere friends.
  4. Greet people with animation & enthusiasm! Say "HELLO" in a tone voice that exudes interest. Be altruistic. Do things for them that require you time, energy and consideration.
  5. Don't hesitate to show interest in others
  6. Before asking someone to do something for you, give them the opportunity to talk about their passions, they'll be very excited to do this, and you'll be able to leverage that enthusiasm and positive energy to ask for what you want.

docs.title: Lesson III: Be A Good Listener. Encourage Others To Talk About Themselves!
docs.text:
1. One can easily make friends by becoming interested in others. Well, being a good listener is a huge of interests.
  2. Being a patient, sympathetic listener is just as important in a individual's personal life as in business.
  3. Be an attentive listener! Show genuine interest by asking questions that will enjoy answering, encourage them to talk about themselves, their goals and their accomplishments.
  4. Do not talk incessantly about yourself, interrupt others when they have something to say.
  5. You should do 25% talking, 75% listening.

docs.title: Lesson IV: To Win Someone To Your Way Of Thinking, Get Them To Say YES Immediately
docs.text:
1. Don't begin by discussing the things on which you disagree. Find common ground and emphasize the things on which you agree.
  2. Get people to say "YES!" right at the beginning, keep them from saying "NO!"
  3. When someone says "NO!", their ego compels them to remain consistent with themselves and stick to it.
  4. You need to get a number of "YESES" responses and move the listeners in the affirmative direction. The "YESES" you can induce, the more likely you are to capture the attention for your ultimate proposal.
  5. Next time you find yourself trying to persuade someone, get them to agree with you and say "YES" to at least 2 things before you each share your perspectives.

docs.title: Lesson V: Ask Question Instead Of Giving Direct Orders
docs.text: Don't forcefully tell people to do , ask them questions that will make them want to do what you expect them to do.

docs.title: Lesson VI: Show Respect For The Other Person's Opinions. Don't Tell Them That They're Wrong!
docs.text:
1. You can tell someone thy are wrong, by a look or a gesture as easily as you can with words, be ware thought.
  2. Telling people they are wrong sends a direct blow to their judgment, intelligence, pride, and self-respect. That will never make them agree with you, it would only make them want to strike back.
  3. It's hard to change people's mind. So don't make it harder by telling them they're wrong. If you're going to correct them, do it subtly, so subtly that no one knows you are doing it.
  4. Most of us tend to be prejudiced, biased, and suspicious when it comes to seeing things from a different point of view. When we are wrong, we might admit it to ourselves. And if we are handled gently, with tactics and diplomacy, we may admit it to others as well, but not if someone is trying to push the unpleasant truth down throat.

docs.title: Lesson VII: Every Time You're The One Who's Wrong, Admit It Quickly And Emphatically
docs.text:
1. Acknowledge your mistakes and admit them before your opponent gets the chance to do it. A kind, forgiving attitude will be taken and your errors will be minimized.

  2. Admitting you error:

    • Shatters any feeling of guilt and defensiveness.
    • Helps fix problem created by by the error.

  3. When you are right, try to win people over gently and tactfully. When you are wrong, admit it readily. Its much more effective than trying to defend yourself.

docs.title: Lesson VIII: Use Encouragement To Empower The Other Person!
docs.text:
1. If you tell someone they are stupid or dump at a certain thing, has no gift for it, and is doing it totally wrong, you will destroy almost every incentive for that person to change or improve.
  2. But if you encourage them and let them know that you trust their ability to do that particular thing or change for better. They won't stop until manage to excel.
  3. Rather than telling someone their goal is out of reach, try to encourage small victories. This will provide both guidance and inspiration, encouraging and praising progress through various incentives or rewards can go a long way!

docs.title: Lesson IX: Talk In Term Of The Other Persons Interest. Make Them feel Happy About Doing Thing You Suggest.
docs.text:
1. People are more likely to cooperate or fulfill your requests if you can show them the benefits in doing so.

  2. How to do it:

    • Don't be selfish.
    • Focus in the other persons interests too.
    • Think about the benefits the benefits the other person should get doing what you suggest.

  3. When you make you request, put it in a way that will convey how it will benefit the other person.

  4. Be honest. Do not promise anything you can not deliver. If anything, it's better to promise less and deliver more.

docs.title: Module 1: Focused and Diffuse Thinking
docs.text:
1. What Is Learning?

    1. Learning is the process of acquiring new knowledge, skills, behaviors, or attitudes through experience, study or instruction.
    2. It involves making connections between different pieces of information and forming a deep understanding of how things work.

  2. How Our Brain Learn?

    1. During Waking: During Waking hours, the brain is constantly processing informations and making new connections.
    2. During Sleep : During sleep, the brain consolidates and strengthens memories, as well as processes new information learned during the wake(The most heavy thinking is done unconscious)

  3. What Are The Modes Of Thinking?

    1. Focused Mode
      • Is a state of deep concentration and intense mental focus on a particular task or activity.
      • It allows you to work efficiently and effectively on a single task without getting sidetracked by other thoughts or distractions.
    2. Diffuse Mode
      • Is a state of mind in which your brain is relaxed and not focused on a particular task (sleep or exercise), allowing you to make connections and generate creative ideas subconsciously.

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
1. Different Types of Memory?

    1. Working Memory
      • Is the part of memory that has to do with what you're immediately and consciously processing in your mind.
      • It can only holds (04) four chunks of information.
    2. Long Term Memory:
      • It's like a storage warehouse.
      • Long term memory is durable yet changeable;

  2. What is the best method to move things from working to long term memory?

    1. Using Spaced Repetition, which is a memory technique that involves reviewing and recalling information at optimal spacing intervals until the information is learned at a sufficient level.

  3. Advices related to learning:

    1. Practice make things permanent.
    2. Doing a little every day is better than all at once.
    3. Doing physical exercise is an important item in the process of learning.

docs.title: Sleep
docs.text:
1. How our sleep effect our learning?
    • Just plain being awake creates toxics products in your brain (Metabolic toxins).
    • During sleep, the brain get rid of this poisons.
    • The sleep, tidies up ideas and concepts that you learn.
    • The sleep erases the less important parts of memories and simultaneously strengthens areas that you need or want to remember.
    • Sleep enhance creativity, problem solving, and cognitive flexibility.

docs.title: Procrastination
docs.text:
1. What is procrastination?

Is the action of unnecessarily and voluntarily delaying or postponing something despite knowing that there will be negative consequences for doing so.

  1. How the procrastination works?

    1. Unhappy
    2. You funnel attention to a more pleasure task (watching mobile...)
    3. Feel happy temporarily

  2. How to overcome procrastination?

    1. Set a timer for 25min (Pomodor Technique).
    2. Be very focus in this 25min.
    3. Take 5min Brake.
    4. Reward your Self.

docs.title: Chunks
docs.text:
1. What is chunks?

Chunks is a cognitive process that involves grouping or organizing pieces of information together in a way that makes easier to remember or process.

  1. How to form a chunk?

    1. Focused attention.
    2. Understanding (the concept (how), context (when)).
    3. Practice.

  2. The important of library of chunks?

    1. Helps to be more creative (Using diffuse, by connecting different chunks)
    2. Helps understand new chunks (Using transfer old learned concept to the new ones)

docs.title: Illusion of competence
docs.text:
1. What is illusion of competence in learning?

It's a phenomenon where individuals overestimate their level of understanding or mastery of a subject, despite their actual level of knowledge or skill.

  1. What causes this phenomenon?

It caused due to relaying on some external storage of information, like a book or google for example.

  1. The solution to this illusion?

    1. Use Recall method

  2. What is Recall?

It is a learning technique that involves retrieving information from memory in order to to strengthen building chunks and enhanced deep learning.

docs.title: Learning
docs.text:
1. The Best Methods to learn?

    1. Combine Recall and spaced repetition : Retrieval Practice
    2. Be motivated
    3. Overlearning
    4. Interleaving
    5. Deliberate practice
    6. Try to create a vivid concepts for an abstract topics.
    7. Try to do explanation (Explain to some thing or some one)
    8. Find a community
    9. The best app for it is Anki

  2. What is Overlearning?

    1. Is a Learning Technique that involves practicing a skill or information beyond the point of mastery.
    2. It is a process of continuing to practice and reinforce a skill or information even after it has been learned to a satisfactory level.

  3. What is Interleaving?

    1. Cognitive strategy that involves alternating between different types of tasks or concepts during practice or learning.
    2. It involves switching between related topics or skills in a systematic way
    3. It helps to develop more flexible and adaptive problem solving skills.
    4. it reduce the effects of forgetting and improving long term retention of information.

  4. What is Deliberate Practice?

    1. Is a type of learning that involves highly focused and intentional efforts to improve performance in a particular skill or domain, it includes:
    2. Focus on a specific goal: Set a specific challenging goals.
    3. Receiving Feedback: Feedback on performance from experts in the field.
    4. Repetition or refinement.
    5. Pushing beyond comfort zone: work on areas of weakness or to challenge your self to perform at a higher level.

  5. How motivation affect the brain?

    1. When you are motivated, your brain releases neurotransmitters and hormones that help us focus our attention, retain information, and remember more effectively.
    2. Acetylcholine: Affects focused learning and attention.
    3. Dopamine: Dopamine signals has relation to unexpected rewards.
    4. Serotonin Affects social life and risk taking behavior.

  6. Bad Practices in learning:

    1. Overlearning ins a single session: is a waste of time.
    2. Einstellung chunking: Refers to the tendency for people to rely on what they know, rather than exploring new possibilities when faced a new problem.

  7. How to enhance your learning?

    1. The best gift to give your mind is Exercise
    2. Use the metaphors and visualization to understand and remember concepts
    3. Always change your thought (Learning) to change your brain
    4. Try to learn your subjects every day for small amount of time, is better than big tile for a single subject each day.
    5. Turn some concepts (wanted to learn) to be remember using the help of relating it with a picture.
    6. For Anki: Before sleep and in the very morning.

  8. Important advices:

    1. In a test:
      • look at all questions
      • Start withe the hard question (give your subconscious a mission) for a moment than switch to easy ones.
    2. In writing
      • Answer this questions for better writing:
        • What Am I Trying To Say?
        • What I Must Not Leave Out?
      • Be clear, the more clear and concious, the more impressive you are.
      • Always separate the editing from writing.
      • Always short sentences are better than long ones.

docs.title: Module 4 : Zombie Mode
docs.text:
1. What is zombie mode?

The zombie mode is the state( routine) where you start doing something (learning...) without thinking so much on how to do it, it only comes fluently.

  • The zombie mode is a great technique to avoid procrastination and free the slots in the front of the head.

  • The secret to improve the zombie mode is to change our habits.

  • What is a habit?

    • the habit had 4 elements:
      1. The Cue (The trigger)
      2. The routine (The zombie mode)
      3. The reward
      4. The belief

  • The Cue

  • Identify that trigger of habit it could be:

    • Location
    • A specific time
    • A Feelings ...

  • The routine

  • Always have a plan

    • Write a weekly list of key tasks
    • Write a daily to do list (better having it a accomplished day before)
    • The plan should have nots of what accomplished and what not
    • Any task should have a starting time and a finishing time.

  • The reward

After any accomplishment, reward your self.

  1. Advices:
    • Focus on building process (A habit), not a product (Only passing the test)
    • Always eat your frog first.

docs.title: What is markdown
docs.text:
• light weight markup languge, with a plain text formating syntax.

docs.title: Markdown Editors:

docs.title: What is Tmux?
docs.text: Tmux is program which runs in a terminal and allows multiple other terminal programs to be run inside it.

docs.title: What Tmux stand for?
docs.text: Tmux stand for terminal multiplexer

docs.title: How tmux manages different programs?
docs.text: Each program inside tmux gets its own terminal manged by tmux, which can be accessed from the single terminal where tmux is running.

docs.title: Can a program be detached from the terminal?
docs.text: Tmux and any programs running inside it can be detached from the terminal where it is running (the outside terminal) and later reattached to the same or another terminal.

docs.title: What means tmux sessions are persistent?
docs.text: Means that programs running in tmux will continue to run even if you get disconnected.

docs.title: What are the main uses of tmux?
docs.text:
• Work with multiple programs and shells together in one terminal, a bit like a window manager.
  • Protect running programs on a remote server from connection drops by running them inside tmux.
  • Allow programs running on remote server to be access from multiple different local computers.

docs.title: When the tmux server start running?
docs.text: The tmux server run automatically, when the server runs a tmux command and by default exists when there are no running programs.

docs.title: Where tmux keep its sates?
docs.text: Tmux keeps all its state in a single main process , called the tmux server. This runs in the background and manges all the programs running inside tmux and keeps track of their output.

docs.title: How the client talks to the tmux server?
docs.text: Tmux takes over the terminal where it is run and talks to the server using a socket file in /tmp

docs.title: Where programs run?
docs.text: Programs run in terminal panes, which each belong to one window

docs.title: What the window has?
docs.text: Each window has a name and one active pane by default.

docs.title: How many sessions windows are linked to?
docs.text: Windows are linked to one or more sessions.

docs.title: How the session keep track of its windows?
docs.text: Each session has a list of windows, each with an index

docs.title: How many clients a session can attache?
docs.text: Sessions are attached to one or more clients, or are detached.

docs.title: What are the commands to use to create a session?
docs.text:
• tmux
  • tmux new
  • tmux new-session

Tipe

All the three commands are used to create a session

docs.title: How to give a session a name?
docs.text: tmux new -smysession : With -s flag you give the session a name

docs.title: How the keys are forwarded once a tmux client is attached?
docs.text: Once a tmux client is attached, any keys entered are forwarded to the program running in the active pane of the current window.

docs.title: What is the default prefix of tmux?
docs.text: C-b

docs.title: What happens when a tmux prefix is pressed?
docs.text: When the prefix key is pressed, tmux waits for another key press and that determines what tmux command is executed.

docs.title: Command to show a list of all the short keys binding?
docs.text: C-b ?

docs.title: How to see a description of single key?
docs.text:
C-b /

Tip

a prompt at the bottom of terminal appears

docs.title: Command to inter the interactive command prompt?
docs.text: C-b :

docs.title: What means detaching from the tmux?
docs.text: Detaching from a tmux session means disconnecting from the session while leaving it running in the background

docs.title: Command to detach?
docs.text: C-b d

docs.title: Command to reattach to the previous session?
docs.text: tmux attach

docs.title: Command to reattach to session by name?
docs.text: tmux attach -tmysession using the -t flag

docs.title: Command to list all the session that can be attached to?
docs.text: tmux ls (ls is an alias tmux-session)

docs.title: How to kill tmux server entirely?
docs.text:
• :kill-server in prompt of tmux
  • or if there are no session, windows or panes inside tmux

docs.title: Command to create new window?
docs.text: C-b c is using the key binding new-window

docs.title: How the pine is created?
docs.text: A pine is created by splitting a window, this done withe split-window command.

docs.title: Command to split the current pane horizontally?
docs.text: C-b %

docs.title: Command to split the current pane vertically?
docs.text: C-b "

docs.title: Command to rotate a pane from horizontally to vertically?
docs.text: C-b space

docs.title: Command to spilt horizontally or vertically a pane? (split-window)
docs.text: split-window -v or split-window -h

docs.title: Command to split the pane without living the active pane? (split-window)
docs.text: split-window -d

docs.title: What does this command do split-window f?
docs.text: Makes a new pane spanning the whole width or height of the window instead of being constrained to the size of the pane being split

docs.title: What does this command do split-window -b?
docs.text: Puts the new pane to left or above of the pane being split instead of the right or below.

docs.title: How to copy using the mouse?
docs.text:
• Shift + selection with the mouse
  • Shift + pressing the middle button of the mouse

docs.title: Command to change to last window?
docs.text: C-b l

docs.title: Command to change to the next window?
docs.text: C-b n

docs.title: command to change to the previous window?
docs.text: C-b p

docs.title: What this command do C-b w?
docs.text: It is used to display a menu of all windows within the current session.

docs.title: What this command do C-b number?
docs.text: It prompts for a window index and change to that window

docs.title: Commands to change between the panes?
docs.text:
• C-b Up
  • C-b Down
  • C-b Left
  • C-b Right

docs.title: What does this command do C-b q?
docs.text: prints the pane numbers and their size on the top the panes for a short time, pressing one of the number keys before they disappear changes the active pane to the chosen pane

docs.title: Command to display list of clients?
docs.text:
C-b D

Tip

Each client is shown in the list in the top half with its name..., entering (d, D, x or X) will detach that client

docs.title: Command to kill a window with confirmation?
docs.text: C-b & bounded to kill-pane and kill-session

docs.title: Command to kill only active pane?
docs.text: C-b x bounded to kill-pane and kill-session

docs.title: Command to rename a session?
docs.text: C-b $

docs.title: Command to rename a window?
docs.text: C-b , bounded to rename-window

docs.title: What this command are used for C-b { and C-b}?
docs.text: Used to swipe pane, they are using swap-window, wsip-pane

docs.title: Commands to resize panes?
docs.text: C-b C-Left, C-b C-Right, C-b C-Up and C-b C-Down

docs.title: Command to make a pane temporarily take the whole window?
docs.text: C-b z ones takes the full window, to back tape the same command

docs.title: Steps to copy using keys?
docs.text:
• C-b [ enter the copy mode
  • C- Space should very fast, used to select
  • C-w to copy
  • C-b ] to paste in the insert mode or the command mode

docs.title: What this commands does in the copy mode q, Ca and C-e?
docs.text:
• q exit the copy mode
  • C-a move the cursor the start of the line
  • C-e move the cursor the end of the line

docs.title: What this commands does in the copy mode C-r, M-f and M-b?
docs.text:
• C-r search interactively backwards
  • M-f, M-b move the cursor to the next or the previous word

docs.title: What this command do C-b =?
docs.text: Offer a list of buffers with a preview of their content

docs.title: What are the keys supported in the buffer mode?
docs.text:
• Enter or P Paste the selected buffer
  • d/D delete the selected buffer

docs.title: Command to create a buffer?
docs.text: :setb -bfoo bar create a buffer named foo, with the content of bar

docs.title: Command to rename a buffer?
docs.text: :setb bbuffer0 -nmybuffer the flag -b gives the existing buffer a name and -n the new name

docs.title: What this command does loadb -bbuffername ~/a/file?
docs.text: load-buffer will load a buffer from a file

docs.title: What this command does saveb -bbuffer0 ~/saved-buffer?
docs.text: An existing buffer can be saved to a file with save-buffer

docs.title: What does this command C-b f?
docs.text: prompts for some text and then enters tree mode with a filter to show only panes where that text appears in the visible content or title of the pane or the window name

docs.title: Command to activate the mouse ?
docs.text: set -g mouse on should typed in .tmux.conf file

docs.title: Waht is Vi
docs.text: Vi is a text editor that's been around for a long time and remains a powerful and widely used tool, especially in Unix-based systems.

docs.title: What are the modes existing in Vi? (04)
docs.text:
• Normal Mode
  • Insert Mode (i)
  • Visual Mode (v)
  • Ex Mode (:)

docs.title: What "normal mode" do?
docs.text: Navigate the structure of the file

docs.title: What "insert mode" do ?
docs.text: Editing the file

docs.title: What "visual mode" do?
docs.text: Highlight portions of the file to manipulate at one

docs.title: What "ex mode" do?
docs.text: Command mode

docs.title: Command to scroll the window down?
docs.text: Ctrl + e or use the arrows

docs.title: Command to scroll the window up?
docs.text: Ctrl + y or use the arrows

docs.title: One single command to move the cursor to the top of the window?
docs.text: H

docs.title: One single command to move the cursor to the middle of the window?
docs.text: M

docs.title: One single command to move the cursor the bottom of the window?
What does gg do?
docs.text: Go to the top of a file

docs.title: What does G do?
docs.text: Go to the end of the file

docs.title: What are the components of the secret sauce?
docs.text:
• Text objects and motions
  • The DOT command
  • macros

docs.title: What are the text objects?
docs.text:
• w word
  • s sentence
  • p paragraph
  • t tag

docs.title: What are the motions in vim?
docs.text:
• a all
  • i in
  • f search forward
  • F search backward

docs.title: What are the essential commands?
docs.text:
• d delete
  • y yank
  • c change
  • v visual selection

docs.title: Describe what this command does, dw daw cw caw?
docs.text:
• dw delete the word starting from the position of the cursor
  • daw delete all the word, doesn't mutter the position of cursor in the word
  • cw delete and put you in the insert mode starting from the position of the cursor.
  • caw: deleting the all word ind puts you in the insert mode

docs.title: Describe what this command does, yi) di) va'?
docs.text:
• yi) yank all text inside parentheses
  • di) delete inside parentheses
  • va' select all inside '

docs.title: What DOT command do?
docs.text: The dot (.) command repeat the last command

docs.title: What this commands does dd/yy D/C ^/$ I/A o/O p/P ?
docs.text:
• dd/yy (delete/yank) the current line
  • D/C (delete/change) all line the end of it
  • ^/$ move to the (beginning/end) of the line
  • I/A move to the (beginning/end) of the line and insert
  • o/O insert new line (above/below) the current
  • p/P paste (below/above)

docs.title: What this commands does u Ctrl+ r b w ?
docs.text:
• u undo
  • Ctrl+ r redo
  • b backward by a word
  • w forward by a word

docs.title: Command to select a line?
docs.text: Shift v

docs.title: Command to back 2 minutes in time?
docs.text: :earlier 2m

docs.title: Command to delete current paragraph?
docs.text: dap

docs.title: Command to yank current paragraph?
docs.text: yap

docs.title: Command to append to current word?
docs.text: ea

docs.title: Command to delete the current function?
docs.text: daf

docs.title: Command to yank current function?
docs.text: yaf

docs.title: What this commands do dif yif?
docs.text:
• dif delete current inner function
  • dif yank current inner function

docs.title: What we called !?
docs.text: They called magic wands or bang bang

docs.title: What uses of the magic wands (!)?
docs.text: Magic wands are mnemonic device for using the exclamation point (!) to send the current line, section or page to any command that can be run from the shell and replace those lines with the output if that command.

docs.title: What are the three types of magic wands?
docs.text:
• Line wand
  • Section wand
  • Line number wand

docs.title: What is the most fastest and common way to extend vi?
docs.text: It is the Line wand

docs.title: How to execute the line wand?
docs.text: Just spam bang twice(!!) to send the current line to the shell command or replace the current line with the output of utility command like date, cal or any text that comes out of any command

docs.title: What are the commands to send a line to bash Calc python3?
docs.text: Just type on the key board : * For bash : !!bash * for Cal : !!bc * for python3!: !!python3

docs.title: How to select a section wand?
docs.text: Just type !}

docs.title: Command to sort a section and send it to bash?
docs.text:
• !}sort
  • !}bash

docs.title: What is a lined number wand?
docs.text: Lined number wand is just a larger version of the section wand but allows blank lines to be included.

docs.title: How to do a lined number wand?
docs.text: Start with bang (type only one bang) and follow it up with a colon and the line after the last line that you want to include fore example: type !, than type : only : colon will appear than add the line number after that one you want to include

docs.title: Give the steps to comment a section with on space?
docs.text:
• Put the cursor where you want start commenting
  • type !} you will select that section, and in the output remove the (!)
  • then type s/^/# /g
  • the full command is :.,.+10s/^/# /g you can not add the (g)

docs.title: Explain this line of code? :.,.+10s/^/# /g
docs.text:
• . dote stand for the current position
  • +10 ten lines plus from the current position
  • s substation
  • ^ the beginning of the line
  • g means change everything similar in that line

docs.title: Give the steps to comment a section with spaces on it?
docs.text:
• put the cursor on the line you want to start from
  • type only one bang (!)
  • type after colon (:) plus the line after the last line that you want to include.
  • The pup of an we line showing the full path of the selected section, type after what you want bash/pandoc or s/^.# (to comment make sure to delete ! before)

docs.title: What this command do h j k l?
docs.text:
• h : move cursor left
  • j : move cursor down
  • k : move cursor up
  • l : move cursor right

docs.title: What this command do H M L?
docs.text:
• H : move to top of screen
  • M : move to middle of screen
  • L : move to bottom of screen

docs.title: What this command do w e b?
docs.text:
• w : jump forwards to start of a word
  • e : jump forwards to end of a word
  • b : jump backwards to start of a word

docs.title: What this commands do 0 $ } { ?
docs.text:
• 0 : jump to the start of the line
  • $ : jump to the end of the line
  • } : jump to next paragraph
  • { : jump to previous paragraph

docs.title: What this commands do zz zt zb C-e C-y?
docs.text:
• zz: centre cursor on screen
  • zt: position cursor on top of the screen
  • zb: position cursor on bottom of the screen
  • Ctrl + e : move screen down
  • Ctrl + y : move screen up one line

docs.title: What this command do i, I, a, A, o, O, ea?
docs.text:
• i: insert before the cursor
  • I: insert at the beginning of the line
  • a: insert (append) after the cursor
  • A: insert (append) at the end of the line
  • o: append (open) a new line below the current line
  • O: append (open) a new line above the current line
  • ea: insert (append) at the end of the word

docs.title: What this commands do C-h Ctrl-w C-j C-t C-d C-n C-p?
docs.text:
• Ctrl + h : delete the character before the cursor during insert mode
  • Ctrl + w : delete word before the cursor during insert mode
  • Ctrl + j : begin new line during insert mode
  • Ctrl + t : indent (move to right) line one shift width during insert mode
  • Ctrl + d : de-indent (move to left) line one shift width during insert mode
  • Ctrl + n : insert (auto-complete) next match before the cursor during insert mode
  • Ctrl + p : insert (auto-complete) previous match before the cursor during insert mode

docs.title: What this commands do r R J gJ?
docs.text:
• r : replace a single character
  • R : replace more than one character, until Esc is pressed
  • J : join line below to the current one with one space in between
  • gJ: join line below to the current one without space in between

docs.title: What this commands do ~ cc C ciw, cw ?
docs.text:
• ~ : change to lower or upper case
  • cc : change (replace) entire line
  • C : change (replace) to the end of the line
  • ciw : change (replace) entire word
  • cw: change (replace) t the end of word

docs.title: What this commands do s S u C-r .?
docs.text:
• s : delete character and substitute text
  • S :delete line and substitute text (same as cc)
  • u : undo
  • Ctrl + r : redo
  • . : repeat last command

docs.title: Command to mark text (visual mode)? (02)
docs.text:
• v : start visual mode, mark lines , then do a command (like y-yank)
  • V : start Line-wise visual mode

docs.title: What this commands in the visual mode > < y d ~ u U?
docs.text:
• > : shift text right
  • < : shift text left
  • y : yank (copy) marked text
  • d : delete marked text
  • ~ : switch case
  • u : change marked text to lower case
  • U : change marked text to uppercase

docs.title: Command to start a macro?
docs.text: qa : record macro a (a is the name)

docs.title: Command to stop recording a macro?
docs.text: q : stop recording macro

docs.title: Command to run a macro?
docs.text:
• @ : run macro a
  • @@ : rerun last run macro

docs.title: Command same as :wq?
docs.text: ZZ

docs.title: Command same as :q!?
docs.text: ZQ

docs.title: Command to delete from the current position into a certain word?
docs.text: d/searchedWord

docs.title: Command to yank from the current position into a certain word?
docs.text: y/searchedWord

docs.title: What this commands do D/d$ dap daf?
docs.text:
• D d$ delete into the end of file
  • dap delete all paragraph
  • daf delete all function

docs.title: Command to delete only what inside the function ?
docs.text: def

docs.title: Command to show where you are?
docs.text: Ctrl+ g

docs.title: what this commands do :% :12?
docs.text:
• :% moves you to the last line
  • :12 moves you to line 12

docs.title: Command to copy the whole file?
docs.text: y :% y is a command flowed by :%

docs.title: Command to delete from current position into the last line?
docs.text: :.,%d

docs.title: Command to select all lines of a paragraph? using!
docs.text: !}

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• Git
  • Content
    • What is Git❓
    • Why use Git❓
    • How Git works❓
    • What is branches ❓
    • Configuration

docs.title: What is Git❓
docs.text:
• Git is free and open source for distribution version control.
  • It's a system that records changes to our files over time.
  • Git allows users to track code changes and manage their project using simple commands.
  • We can recalls specific version of those files at any given time.
  • Many people can easily collaborate on a project and have their own version of project files on their computer.

docs.title: Why use Git❓
docs.text:
• Store revisions in a project history in just one directory.
  • Rewind on new features in the project I wanted to.
  • Work on a new feature without messing up the main codebase.
  • Easily collaborate with other programmers.

docs.title: How Git works❓
docs.text:
• The hart of Git is repository used to contain a project.
  • What is Repository ?
  • A repository is a container for a project you want to track with git.
  • A repository can allow users to store several different repo's and truck each one dependently.
  • You can have many different repos's for many different projects on your computer.
  • What is commits ?
  • Git commit, creates a commit, which is like a snapshot of your repository.
  • commits are the building blocks of a 'save points' with in git's version control.
  • This commit are snapshots of your entire repository to a specific times.
  • You should make new commits often, based around logical unites of change.
  • Over time commits should tell us a story of the history of your repo and how it came to be they way it currently is.
  • What is the commit process ?
  • The Git committing process requires several steps: Moving Changes to the staging area and Saving them with Commit Command.
  • The phases of a commit:
    1. Modified: Changed files not commited.
    2. Staging: Add any changed files to staging that you want to commit.
    3. Commited: Any files in the stagin area are added to the commit when we make one.

docs.title: What is branches ❓
docs.text:
• A branch is new/separate version of the main repo.
  • Branches allow you to work on different parts of a project, without impacting the main branch.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
1. some basic commands in git:
sudo apt install git  #used to insall git on ubuntu.
git --version         #display the version.
git config --global user.name soufiyane             #to set a username to the git.
git config --gloabal user.email soufiyane@gmail.com #to set an email to git.
git config user.name  #display the username.
git config user.email #dispaly the email.

  1. How to create and manipulate repos ?

  2. Create a new repo:

mkdir my-project` #create a folder for the project
cd my-project     #go to the folder
git init          #intialize a new, empty repo here
  • How to commit a change ?
git add filename       #add the changed file to the staged phase
git add .              #add all files to the staging phase
git rm --cach filename #to remove a file from the stage phase
git status             #display the status file in stage phase(if add or not)
git commit -m "describ the current commit" #now you'r committing, with a discription message
git log                #display all the commits in the long format
git log --oneline      #display all the commits in a concise format

  • How to back and change the commits ?

  • checkout a commit : View the commits without changing anything (read only)

git checkout 3243FFD #view the all the commits specific time, without editing(read only)

 ```

1. revert a commit: Basically it can modify a commit, but it creates a new one, withe the new or deleted feature

```bash
git revert 233EDS #the reverted commit steel in the logs
  1. reset a commit: it means deleting the commit for good
  2. deleting the commit, with the possibility to recover
git reset 3242ffs #stell we have the changing in the working area, to recover:
    git add . 
    git commit -m "undo the reset"
  1. deleting for good(no chance to recover)
git reset 12131 --hard #remove the commit defentlly

  1. How to manipulate branches:

  2. create new branch and move to it

git bransh feature-1   # Create a new branch
git branch -a          # Display all the branches
git checkout feature-1 # Switch to a specific branch
  • How to delete a branch?
git checkout master     # You should move to master/main first
git branch -d feature-1 # Delete a merged branch
git branch -D feature-1 # Delete un merged branch
  • How to merge a branch?
git checkout master # Move to the branch that you want to marge in to it
git merge feature-1 # Merge the feature 1 to master branch
  • What is conflict?
  • An example of a conflict, when you make a new Branch for new feature, then after you a guy modifies in the master branch, then you need to help Git to decide which modification to keep
git marge feature-1 # pops an error, you need modifie the code
git add .           # add to stagin erea
git commit`         # make a commit witout a name(:wq, if a bash file is popeds)

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• Github
  • Content
    • What is Github❓
    • what is token ❓
    • What is Forking \& contributing
    • configuration
    • How to create and manage a project on Github ❓
  • fetch vs pull

docs.title: What is Github❓
docs.text:
• online service that host our project.
  • share our code with other developers.
  • developers can download the projects and work on theme.
  • they can re-upload their edits and marge them with the main code base.

docs.title: what is token ❓
docs.text:
• personnel access token are alternative to using password for authentication to Github when using CLI or Github API
  • a token is no different from a password except:
  • it's near-guaranteed to never used else where
  • it's guaranteed to have high entropy
  • it's scope is more limited than an account-wide password
  • in short, Github took things out of the hand of users, because users make poor security decisions.

docs.title: What is Forking & contributing
docs.text: • Forking is a process when you copy a repo that is owned by someone else and he made it public to your forked repos.

docs.title: How to create and manage a project on Github ❓
docs.text:
1. First scenario: you are building the project for the first time

  2. create a repo

  3. copy the URL of you repo
  4. go to you terminal: git clone URL
  5. after any change and committing the project you can update the project on Github: git push URL master(mater or main)
  6. in general when you clone the Github creates an alias to the URL to verify: git remote -v so to update you type only : git push origin main(you need a username of your Github, and token)
git clone https/...  # Copy the project(files) in my local machine
git remote -v        # Display the alias of the url
git push url main    # Update the project with url
git push origin main # Update the project using the alias

  1. second scenario: you have an existing project in your local pc

  2. you should create a repo, withe the exact name as your project

  3. copy the link to the repo
  4. go to terminal
  5. verify if everything is committed git status
  6. then push to Github : git push URL main
  7. you can create an alias to the URL : git remote add origin URL

  8. then send:git push origin main

  9. collaborating on a team

  10. collaboration, means how to work with Github on a team

  11. first we suppose we have project on git hub
  12. after working on the project locally make sure to update from the remote: git pull origin main
  13. create a new branch for the new feature, never work directly on the main(master) : git checkout -b feature-1 #this command create and move at the same time to the branch
  14. send the new branch: git push origin feature-1

  15. then i create a pull request, my work need to approved it and reviewed it by team members or the manger, only than i can merge the branch to main or the master

  16. How to fork ❓

  17. click 'fork' in the original repo
  18. in your forked repo, click the clone or download button
  19. copy the git link : git clone URL
  20. How to update from the originals?
  21. git remote add upstream <origin-repo-add>
  22. git fetch upstream
  23. git pull upstream master: to update the local master
  24. Why to submit a pull request?
  25. once you have made changes to your fork, you can request for those changes to be merged into the original project.

docs.title: fetch vs pull
docs.text:
1. fetch vs pull
  2. both are used to download new data from a remote repo
  3. fetch git fetch upstream master
    • git fetch really only downloads new data from a remote repo, but it doesn't integrate this new data into your working files
    • fetch is a greate for getting first view on all things that happend in a remote repo
  4. pull git pull upstream master
    • git pull, in contrast, is used with a different goal in lined: to update your corrent HEAD branch with the latest changes from the remote server.
